race.name: RBC GranFondo Whistler
race.tagline: 122 km from Vancouver to Whistler on a fully closed Sea-to-Sky Highway. North America's largest gran fondo — 5,000+ riders crossing the Lions Gate Bridge at sunrise and climbing through the Coast Mountains on the one day a year this highway belongs to bikes.

vitals.location: Vancouver to Whistler, British Columbia, Canada
vitals.registration: Online via rbcgranfondo.com. CAD $200-300 (~$145-220 USD) depending on distance category and registration tier. Early-bird pricing available. Under-19 discounted at $50.
vitals.feed_zones: 4 official aid stations spaced approximately 25 km apart starting at Britannia Beach (km 51). Water, electrolytes, energy food, fruit. On-course entertainment and medical support throughout.
vitals.cutoff_time: Approximately 8.5 hours for the GranFondo. Full course support with SAG vehicles and sweeper.
route_options: 152 km Forte (~95 mi, 3,100m / 10,170 ft climbing — adds Cypress Mountain climb)
122 km GranFondo (~76 mi, 2,300m / 7,546 ft climbing — the classic route)
55 km Medio (~34 mi, 835m / 2,740 ft climbing — Whistler-based, e-bike friendly)

climate.primary: Pacific Northwest late summer — mild, variable, coastal-to-alpine transition
climate.description: Early September in the Sea-to-Sky corridor is typically the tail end of British Columbia's dry season. Vancouver starts cool at 50-60F (10-16C) in the early morning, warming to 60-75F (16-24C) through the day. Whistler sits higher and cooler. The route transitions from coastal to alpine climate across 122 km. Rain is possible — this is the Pacific Northwest — but September is statistically one of the driest months. Morning fog along Howe Sound burns off. The second half from Squamish to Whistler can bring afternoon headwinds and heat radiating off the highway.
challenges: Early morning start in cool Vancouver (50-55F) with potential fog on Howe Sound
Temperature differential between sea-level start and mountain finish
Possible afternoon headwinds in the Squamish-to-Whistler corridor
Rain is always possible in the Pacific Northwest — even in September's dry window
Heat radiation off highway pavement in the second half on warm days

terrain.primary: Fully paved Sea-to-Sky Highway — coastal then mountain climbing
terrain.surface: Smooth paved highway throughout. The Sea-to-Sky Highway was significantly upgraded for the 2010 Winter Olympics, making it one of the best-maintained stretches of road in British Columbia. The first half is coastal and rolling along Howe Sound with some punchy climbs at Furry Creek and Britannia Beach. The second half from Squamish to Whistler is a sustained mountain ascent through the Coast Range. All roads fully closed to traffic for the event — a dedicated car-free lane the entire way.
features: Lions Gate Bridge crossing at sunrise — iconic Vancouver landmark
Howe Sound coastal riding with ocean and mountain views
Sea-to-Sky Highway — fully closed to traffic, Olympic-upgraded surface
Furry Creek and Britannia Beach punch climbs
Sustained Squamish-to-Whistler mountain ascent through the Coast Range
Whistler Village finish — resort town atmosphere

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 61. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 60.

course_description.character: A point-to-point journey from sea level to the mountains along one of North America's most scenic highways. The course starts in Vancouver's Stanley Park, crosses the Lions Gate Bridge, and follows the Sea-to-Sky Highway north along Howe Sound's coastal fjord before turning inland at Squamish to climb through the Coast Mountains to Whistler Village. The first half is coastal and rolling — dramatic ocean-and-mountain views with punchy climbs at Furry Creek and Britannia Beach. The second half is the grind — a sustained ascent from Squamish through the Cheakamus Valley and Garibaldi Highlands, where the ocean recedes and the mountains close in. The highway is fully closed to traffic, giving riders a car-free lane on pavement that was resurfaced for the 2010 Winter Olympics. The finish in Whistler Village at 670 meters elevation feels earned — you started at the ocean and climbed through the clouds to get here.
course_description.signature_challenge: The second half — from Squamish to Whistler. The coastal scenery fades, the sustained climbing begins, and the relentless 2-4% grade through the Cheakamus Valley separates the trained from the hopeful. Most of the 2,300m of climbing loads into the final 60 km.
suffering_zones: Stanley Park Start & Lions Gate Bridge

logistics.airport: Vancouver International Airport (YVR) — major international hub with direct flights from across North America, Europe, and Asia-Pacific. Located approximately 30 minutes from downtown Vancouver and the Stanley Park start area.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Point-to-point complicates accommodation. Most riders stay in Vancouver the night before (downtown hotels near Stanley Park: CAD $200-350/night) and either bus back to Vancouver post-race or stay in Whistler Saturday night (CAD $250-400/night at resort pricing). Book Whistler accommodation months in advance — event weekend books out fast. Tourism Whistler is the official accommodation partner. Budget-conscious riders can stay in Squamish (halfway point, more affordable) and shuttle to the start. Some riders book two nights in Whistler and take the shuttle to Vancouver for the start.
logistics.food: Vancouver and Whistler are both excellent food cities. Pre-race carb loading in Vancouver: countless pasta, sushi, and West Coast restaurants downtown. Post-race in Whistler Village: the finish festival includes beer, wine, and hot food. Whistler's restaurant scene ranges from casual pub fare to fine dining. Coffee culture is strong in both cities — flat whites and pour-overs are the Pacific Northwest way.

history.origin_story: Born in the euphoria following the 2010 Vancouver Winter Olympics, the GranFondo Whistler was conceived to put the Sea-to-Sky Highway — the corridor that had carried the world to the Whistler Olympic venues — back in the spotlight. The idea was audacious: close one of British Columbia's most critical transportation arteries for an entire day and let thousands of cyclists ride it car-free. RBC signed on as title sponsor early, lending the event corporate credibility and financial muscle. The first edition drew enthusiastic crowds, and the event quickly grew into North America's largest gran fondo. The Sea-to-Sky Highway's Olympic-era upgrades — widened lanes, improved surface, new safety features — made it an ideal cycling venue. What started as a post-Olympic celebration became an annual institution.
notable_moments: 2010: Inaugural event launched following the Vancouver Winter Olympics, leveraging the upgraded Sea-to-Sky Highway
RBC (Royal Bank of Canada) signs on as title sponsor, establishing corporate-backed cycling tradition
Multiple 'Best Gran Fondo in North America' awards (2017, 2018, 2019, 2023, 2024)
2022: Event returns post-COVID with renewed energy and strong participation
2024: Record-breaking 15th anniversary — 5,245 registered riders from 48 countries, 14,000+ riders and spectators
2024: First paracyclist completes the 152 km Forte distance
Forte distance category added — 152 km with Cypress Mountain climb before joining the GranFondo route
Generates over CAD $5 million annually for British Columbia's economy
history.reputation: North America's largest and most awarded gran fondo. The gold standard for mass-participation cycling events on the continent.

biased_opinion.summary: There is one day a year when the Sea-to-Sky Highway — British Columbia's most famous road, the corridor that connects Vancouver's glass towers to Whistler's snow-capped peaks — belongs entirely to bicycles. That day is GranFondo day. You roll out of Stanley Park at dawn. You cross the Lions Gate Bridge with 5,000 riders and the sun rising over the North Shore. You ride along Howe Sound's fjord-blue water with mountains falling into the ocean on both sides. You climb through the Coast Range on pavement so smooth and so empty of cars that you forget this is normally a highway. You finish in Whistler Village, where a beer and a party and the disbelief that you just did that are all waiting. The RBC GranFondo Whistler is not a hard-man's race. It's not technical, it's not remote, and it won't destroy you. But it will give you 122 km of closed-highway riding through some of the most spectacular scenery in North America, and that experience — the sheer, rare luxury of a car-free highway through the mountains — is something you simply cannot get any other way.
strengths: Fully closed Sea-to-Sky Highway: the one day a year this iconic road belongs to bikes, not cars
World-class scenery: Lions Gate Bridge, Howe Sound, Coast Mountains, Whistler Village
Premium organization: 15+ years of refinement, RBC sponsorship, award-winning production
Accessible challenge: 2,300m of climbing is demanding but achievable for trained recreational riders
Whistler finish: ending at a world-class resort village makes the post-ride experience memorable
weaknesses: Not a race: competitive depth is limited — this is a participatory event with timed categories
Expensive destination: Vancouver and Whistler hotel costs make this a premium-budget weekend
Technicality is zero: if you want anything beyond smooth highway, look elsewhere
Weather uncertainty: the Pacific Northwest can always deliver rain, even in September
Point-to-point logistics: you need accommodation strategy for two cities 122 km apart
biased_opinion.bottom_line: 5,000 riders on a closed highway through the Coast Mountains. The scenery does the talking, the organization does the heavy lifting, and you do the climbing.

final_verdict.one_liner: 122 km on a closed highway from Vancouver to Whistler. The Sea-to-Sky belongs to bikes for one day — and it's magnificent.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want one of North America's premier mass-participation cycling experiences with world-class scenery and bulletproof organization. Yes if riding a closed highway through the Coast Mountains sounds like your idea of paradise. Skip if you want a competitive road race, technical terrain, or a budget-friendly weekend.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want a Canadian gran fondo with more competition: try the Banff Gran Fondo. If you want Sea-to-Sky riding without the event: ride Highway 99 any summer day (with traffic). If you want a similar-scale European gran fondo: try the Maratona dles Dolomites.

videos.title: RBC GranFondo Whistler Official Video
videos.description:
Speedboats, drone, multiple Helijet helicopters, camera cars: the making of the 2018 RBC GranFondo Whistler event video had it all. Follow our hero Bejan as he takes on North America's best voted Gran Fondo from start to finish.

Learn more and register now: http://on.rbcgranfondo.com/trk/learn

Created by Alterna Films in association with Tourism Whistler, Resort Municipality of Whistler, Tourism Vancouver, Destination BC and RBC GranFondo Whistler. With thanks to the Province of British Columbia.

videos.title: 2025 RBC Grand Fondo Vancouver to Whistler
RBC GranFondo Whistler 15 years: One year for one day.
videos.description:
Get an inside look at how the RBC GranFondo Whistler comes together, from both the event organizers and riders' perspectives.

Don’t miss out on the adventure—register now for the 2025 ride on September 6th!

Register here:   https://on.rbcgranfondo.com/trk/registerweb

videos.title: Whistler Gran Fondo 2024 [FULL]
videos.description:
GoPro recording of the 2024 Whistler Gran Fondo (122km). Official time: 3:54:55
---
8:45 Lions Gate Bridge
13:25 Taylor Way Climb
16:30 Highway 1 to Horseshoe Bay
32:50 The Bridge of Many Flats
35:00 Horseshoe Bay / Rest Stop #1
50:05 Lions Bay
57:50 Descent to Brunswick Beach

01:16:00 Furry Creek Climb

01:37:30 Stawamus Chief
01:37:45 Squamish Obstacle Course

01:56:20 Alice Lake climb
02:05:10 Rest Stop #3

02:15:55 Cheakamus climb
02:22:00 Tantalus lookout descent
02:37:10 Caught by the 2nd big group
02:41:52 Rest Stop #4

03:19:10 Brandywine Falls / Rest Stop #5
03:44:30 Arriving to Whistler area
03:54:00 Whistler Village
03:54:20 Sprint to the finish line
videos.curation_reason: Complete first-person race ride-along with excellent view count and detailed course markers including key climbs and landmarks.

key_challenges.name: Whistler logistics setup
key_challenges.description: Event organizers have to be in and out pretty quickly in Whistler so they don't have a whole lot of time to have the entire thing set up, execute the operation and then pull it all down basically the same day of the event

additional_quotes.text: It really is a full-time job because basically we started work the day after the grand Fondo is finished for the next year

Throughout the winter and through the spring I've had one or two rides in a week usually what that looks like is one kind of shorter ride in the middle of the week so 50 to 80k and then on the weekend it's usually over 100 km with friends

We pride ourselves about being about participation and doing the journey however long it takes and everybody gets the same experience if you're cardio or if you're athletic enough to do it faster than it's great but we really care about the never ridden just started

rider_intel.search_text: The RBC GranFondo Whistler is described as an inclusive participation-focused event that welcomes riders of all abilities, from experienced cyclists to those who have never ridden or just started. The event requires extensive year-round planning with organizers working full-time starting the day after each event to prepare for the next year. Logistically, the event presents challenges as organizers must set up, execute, and tear down operations in Whistler on the same day due to time constraints. Participants describe taking time to train and enjoy the views while pushing themselves to feel accomplished. The event emphasizes participation over speed, ensuring all riders get the same experience regardless of their fitness level or completion time. Training approaches mentioned include one to two rides per week with shorter midweek rides of 50-80km and longer weekend rides over 100km with friends.
